title: Knowledge Check: Sugar
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 4: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the larger questions, how a craving for sweetness drove the slave trade and what we owe that history, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"Sugar comes from two different plants. Which pair is correct?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Sugar cane, a tropical grass, and sugar beet, a temperate root"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Two kinds of tropical tree"},{"id":"q1c","text":"A cactus and a vine"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Corn and wheat"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Sugar's sucrose comes from sugar cane, a tropical grass, and sugar beet, a temperate root that needs a winter freeze. They grow in opposite climates but make the same sweetener.","citation":"Lesson: Two Plants, One Sweetness"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Why did sugar cane plantations need a mill on site and a fast, total harvest?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Cut cane spoils within about 24 hours, so it must be pressed almost immediately"},{"id":"q2b","text":"Cane is too heavy to move at all"},{"id":"q2c","text":"The law required on-site mills"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Cane must be frozen before pressing"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Cut cane breaks down within about 24 hours, so the mill sat on site and the harvest had to be fast and total. That clock helped drive the demand for a large, controllable workforce.","citation":"Lesson: Two Plants, One Sweetness"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"How does sugar beet differ from sugar cane in storage and water use?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Beet can be stored for months and uses about half the water"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Beet spoils faster and needs more water"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Beet cannot be stored at all"},{"id":"q3d","text":"They are identical in both"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Sugar beet can be stored for months before processing and uses about half the water of cane, so it does not need an on-site mill or a 24-hour harvest. That flexibility suited temperate, mechanized farming.","citation":"Lesson: Two Plants, One Sweetness"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"Which country grows the most sugar, about 20 percent of the world's supply?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Brazil"},{"id":"q4b","text":"The United States"},{"id":"q4c","text":"France"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Cuba"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Brazil grows about 20 percent of the world's sugar, the most of any country. India is second at about 18 percent. The United States produces about 5 percent.","citation":"Lesson: Where Sugar Grows"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"How does the United States make its sugar?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"About 55 percent from beet in the north and 45 percent from cane in the south, divided by the frost line"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Entirely from cane"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Entirely from beet"},{"id":"q5d","text":"It imports all of its sugar"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"The United States makes about 55 percent of its sugar from beet (northern states) and 45 percent from cane (Florida, Louisiana). The frost line divides cane country from beet country.","citation":"Lesson: Where Sugar Grows"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What does it mean that a Caribbean sugar island was a monocrop economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Almost all its land and labor went to a single crop, cane, replacing forests and food farms"},{"id":"q6b","text":"It grew exactly one of every crop"},{"id":"q6c","text":"It grew no crops at all"},{"id":"q6d","text":"It rotated many crops each year"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"A monocrop economy devotes nearly all land and labor to one crop. Sugar replaced forests and food farming on the islands, creating great wealth, great suffering, and lasting fragility.","citation":"Lesson: The Sugar Islands"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Which colony was the most profitable in the world by 1789?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Saint-Domingue, now Haiti"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Barbados"},{"id":"q7c","text":"Cuba"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Brazil"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Saint-Domingue, the French colony that is now Haiti, was the single most profitable colony in the world by 1789, producing around 163,000 tonnes of sugar a year on enslaved labor.","citation":"Lesson: The Sugar Islands"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"Where and roughly when was sugar cane first grown?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"New Guinea, around 8000 BCE"},{"id":"q8b","text":"The Caribbean, around 1500 CE"},{"id":"q8c","text":"England, around 1700"},{"id":"q8d","text":"Brazil, around 1000 CE"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Sugar cane was first grown in New Guinea around 8000 BCE. It spread slowly westward over thousands of years before crossing the Atlantic.","citation":"Lesson: From New Guinea to the New World"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"Who brought sugar cane to the New World, and in what year?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Columbus, in 1493"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Cortes, in 1519"},{"id":"q9c","text":"The British, in 1640"},{"id":"q9d","text":"The Aztec, in 1345"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Columbus brought sugar cane to Hispaniola in 1493, on his second voyage, beginning New World sugar. Within about 150 years it became a mass commodity grown by enslaved people.","citation":"Lesson: From New Guinea to the New World"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What was the triangular trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"A three-leg Atlantic loop: goods to Africa, enslaved people to the Americas, and sugar back to Europe"},{"id":"q10b","text":"A trade route between three European cities"},{"id":"q10c","text":"A deal among three sugar companies"},{"id":"q10d","text":"A three-day shipping schedule"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The triangular trade was a three-leg Atlantic loop. Ships carried goods to West Africa, enslaved Africans to the Americas on the Middle Passage, and sugar, rum, and molasses back to Europe.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade and the Middle Passage"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"On a 1750 sugar plantation, what was the single largest startup cost?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"The enslaved people themselves, about half of the total investment"},{"id":"q11b","text":"The sugar mill"},{"id":"q11c","text":"The land"},{"id":"q11d","text":"The buildings"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"The largest startup cost was the enslaved people themselves, about half of the total investment. The system treated human beings as property and as the main capital of the business.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade and the Middle Passage"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"About what share of the Caribbean sugar islands' population was enslaved around 1750?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"About 85 percent"},{"id":"q12b","text":"About 10 percent"},{"id":"q12c","text":"About 40 percent"},{"id":"q12d","text":"About 1 percent"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Around 1750, enslaved Africans were about 85 percent of the Caribbean sugar islands' population. The islands were majority enslaved, run by a small number of planters and overseers.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade and the Middle Passage"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What were maroon communities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Independent settlements of people who escaped slavery, formed in mountains and forests"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Sugar-trading companies"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Plantation owners' associations"},{"id":"q13d","text":"A type of sugar mill"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Maroon communities were independent settlements of people who escaped slavery, formed in the mountains and forests. They were one of the organized forms of resistance to the plantation system.","citation":"Lesson: Resistance and Revolution"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What made the Haitian Revolution unique in the history of slavery?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"It was the only successful large-scale slave revolt, founding a free Haiti in 1804"},{"id":"q14b","text":"It was quickly defeated"},{"id":"q14c","text":"It was led by European planters"},{"id":"q14d","text":"It had no lasting effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Enslaved people in Saint-Domingue rose up in 1791 and, after thirteen years of war, founded the free nation of Haiti in 1804. It was the only successful large-scale slave revolt in history.","citation":"Lesson: Resistance and Revolution"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"How did slavery on the British sugar plantations end?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"In stages: the slave trade abolished in 1807, slavery in 1833, but with a forced unpaid apprenticeship first"},{"id":"q15b","text":"All at once in 1807, with full freedom immediately"},{"id":"q15c","text":"It never legally ended"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Planters freely chose to end it in 1750"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Britain abolished the slave trade in 1807 and slavery itself in 1833, but forced a period of unpaid apprenticeship first. The end came in stages that were fought for, not freely given.","citation":"Lesson: Resistance and Revolution"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"After abolition, how did planters keep the sugar plantations running?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"With indentured laborers, more than 500,000 from India under harsh contracts"},{"id":"q16b","text":"By fully mechanizing overnight"},{"id":"q16c","text":"By shutting the plantations down"},{"id":"q16d","text":"By paying high free wages immediately"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Planters brought in indentured laborers, more than 500,000 from India alone, under restrictive contracts, to replace enslaved labor and keep the plantations running.","citation":"Lesson: Resistance and Revolution"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"How did yearly sugar consumption change from 1700 to today?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"From about 4 pounds a person to about 130 pounds a person"},{"id":"q17b","text":"It barely changed"},{"id":"q17c","text":"It fell sharply"},{"id":"q17d","text":"From about 130 pounds to about 4 pounds"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Yearly sugar consumption rose from about 4 pounds a person in 1700 to about 130 pounds today, a huge increase that tracked sugar's fall in price.","citation":"Lesson: From Luxury to the Cheapest Calorie"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What does price elasticity of demand describe, using sugar as the example?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"How consumption changes with price; a roughly 95 percent price drop brought a roughly 3,150 percent rise in use"},{"id":"q18b","text":"How sweet a product tastes"},{"id":"q18c","text":"How far sugar can be shipped"},{"id":"q18d","text":"The legal price of sugar"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Price elasticity of demand is how much consumption changes when price changes. As sugar's price fell about 95 percent, consumption rose about 3,150 percent.","citation":"Lesson: From Luxury to the Cheapest Calorie"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What is a negative externality, in the case of cheap sugar?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"A cost not in the price, such as about 190 billion dollars a year in health costs"},{"id":"q19b","text":"A discount for buying in bulk"},{"id":"q19c","text":"A tax paid by farmers"},{"id":"q19d","text":"The sweetness of the product"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"A negative externality is a cost not reflected in the market price. Overconsumption of sugar is linked to about 190 billion dollars a year in diabetes, obesity, and dental costs, paid by taxpayers, insurers, and patients.","citation":"Lesson: The Hidden Costs of Cheap Sugar"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How does the U.S. sugar program affect prices?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Quotas, price supports, and tariffs keep U.S. prices about 40 percent above world prices"},{"id":"q20b","text":"It makes U.S. sugar the cheapest in the world"},{"id":"q20c","text":"It has no effect on prices"},{"id":"q20d","text":"It bans all domestic sugar"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"The U.S. sugar program uses import quotas, price supports, and tariffs that keep domestic prices about 40 percent above world prices, costing consumers about 3.5 billion dollars a year while protecting about 4,000 farmers and about 140,000 jobs.","citation":"Lesson: The Hidden Costs of Cheap Sugar"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What is downstream displacement, in the sugar economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Higher protected sugar prices push food manufacturers to move production overseas"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Sugar flowing downriver to ports"},{"id":"q21c","text":"Farmers moving to cities"},{"id":"q21d","text":"A drop in sugar quality over time"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Downstream displacement is the unintended job loss from protection. Because the sugar program raises input costs, some candy and food makers move production overseas, so sugar-farming jobs are protected while food-manufacturing jobs are exported.","citation":"Lesson: The Hidden Costs of Cheap Sugar"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"The word 'sugar' traces which path?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"Sanskrit sharkara to Arabic sukkar to Old French sucre"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Latin to Greek to English"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Nahuatl to Spanish to English"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Chinese to Russian to English"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Sugar comes from Sanskrit sharkara, through Arabic sukkar, to Old French sucre. The word's path traces the crop's spread from India to the Middle East to Europe.","citation":"Lesson: The Words of Sugar"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What did the colonial trade term 'muscovado' mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Unfinished, from Portuguese, used for unrefined sugar"},{"id":"q23b","text":"A type of ship"},{"id":"q23c","text":"A Caribbean island"},{"id":"q23d","text":"A unit of money"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Muscovado comes from the Portuguese for unfinished, a colonial trade term for unrefined sugar. The everyday vocabulary of sugar, like molasses and hogshead, is a fossil of the trade.","citation":"Lesson: The Words of Sugar"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"How does William Beckford's planter account describe the plantation, and what is the ELA lesson?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"With euphemism and passive voice that sanitize forced labor; readers learn to spot bias in 'neutral' description"},{"id":"q24b","text":"With brutal honesty about the violence"},{"id":"q24c","text":"With no mention of labor at all"},{"id":"q24d","text":"As a first-person enslaved witness"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Beckford, a wealthy planter, used euphemism and passive voice that hide the violence of forced labor. Reading him teaches how seemingly neutral language can carry bias.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice Tells the Story"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"What makes the narratives of Olaudah Equiano and Mary Prince important as texts?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"They are first-person witness accounts by formerly enslaved writers, a counter-narrative to planter accounts"},{"id":"q25b","text":"They were written by plantation owners"},{"id":"q25c","text":"They are works of fiction"},{"id":"q25d","text":"They avoid the subject of slavery"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Equiano and Mary Prince wrote first-person witness accounts. Prince's was the first published account by an enslaved Caribbean woman, and her declaration that the truth ought to be told answers the planters' silence.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice Tells the Story"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"What is Jane Austen's 'strategic silence' about sugar in Mansfield Park?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Sugar wealth appears in the novel but is never examined, so the reader must read for what is left out"},{"id":"q26b","text":"The novel is entirely about sugar plantations"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Austen condemns the sugar trade directly"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Sugar is never mentioned at all"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"In Mansfield Park, the family's sugar wealth is present but unexamined. That strategic silence teaches reading for absence, noticing what a text leaves out.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice Tells the Story"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What does the abolitionist phrase 'blood-sweetened luxury' do as language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"It is a compound adjective that fuses sweetness and violence to make consumption a moral act"},{"id":"q27b","text":"It describes a new flavor of sugar"},{"id":"q27c","text":"It praises the sugar trade"},{"id":"q27d","text":"It is a brand name"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"The 1791 boycott petition's phrase blood-sweetened luxury is a compound adjective that fuses sweetness with the violence behind it, turning buying sugar into a moral choice.","citation":"Lesson: Blood-Sweetened Luxury"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Which appeals did Thomas Clarkson use to argue against the slave trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Ethos, pathos, and logos, connecting a shopper's choice to the whole system"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Only humor"},{"id":"q28c","text":"Only statistics, with no moral argument"},{"id":"q28d","text":"He made no argument"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Clarkson used the classic persuasive appeals, ethos, pathos, and logos, with cause-and-effect reasoning that linked everyday consumer behavior to systemic harm.","citation":"Lesson: Blood-Sweetened Luxury"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"About how large is the global sugar market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"About 185 to 220 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q29b","text":"About 2 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q29c","text":"About 18 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q29d","text":"About 5 trillion dollars a year"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"The global sugar market is worth about 185 to 220 billion dollars a year. Sugar became one of the cheapest and most widely used commodities on Earth.","citation":"Lesson: From Luxury to the Cheapest Calorie"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"About how far did sugar's price fall as consumption soared, and what does that show?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Roughly a 95 percent price drop paired with a roughly 3,150 percent rise in consumption"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Prices rose as people ate less"},{"id":"q30c","text":"Prices and consumption both stayed flat"},{"id":"q30d","text":"A small price change with a huge drop in use"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Sugar's price fell about 95 percent while consumption rose about 3,150 percent, a textbook case of elastic demand: as a good gets much cheaper, people use far more of it.","citation":"Lesson: From Luxury to the Cheapest Calorie"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Which sugar plant needs a winter freeze to grow well?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"Sugar beet"},{"id":"q31b","text":"Sugar cane"},{"id":"q31c","text":"Both"},{"id":"q31d","text":"Neither"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Sugar beet grows in cool temperate places and needs a winter freeze, the opposite of cane, which dies at frost. That is why beet lets temperate countries make their own sugar.","citation":"Lesson: Two Plants, One Sweetness"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What share of people died on the Middle Passage, on average?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"About 15 percent"},{"id":"q32b","text":"About 1 percent"},{"id":"q32c","text":"About 50 percent"},{"id":"q32d","text":"None"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"About 15 percent of enslaved people died on the Middle Passage, the brutal six-to-eight-week Atlantic crossing. The cheap sugar of the third leg rested on the suffering of the second.","citation":"Lesson: The Triangular Trade and the Middle Passage"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"Where was sugar first refined into storable crystal sugar, around 500 CE?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"India"},{"id":"q33b","text":"England"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Brazil"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Cuba"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Around 500 CE, people in India worked out how to refine cane juice into crystal sugar that could be stored and shipped, a key step in sugar's spread.","citation":"Lesson: From New Guinea to the New World"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What is full-cost accounting, applied to sugar?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Including all externalities, so if health costs were in the price, sugar would cost far more than about 2 dollars a pound"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Counting only the farm-gate price"},{"id":"q34c","text":"Ignoring all costs except shipping"},{"id":"q34d","text":"A government subsidy"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Full-cost accounting includes all the externalities in a price. If sugar's price included the health costs of overconsumption, it would be far higher than its current low price.","citation":"Lesson: The Hidden Costs of Cheap Sugar"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Which Caribbean island's 1640s boom set the plantation model the others copied?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"Barbados"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Cuba"},{"id":"q35c","text":"Jamaica"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Haiti"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Barbados boomed first, in the 1640s, introducing enslaved labor at scale and setting the Caribbean plantation model that Jamaica, Saint-Domingue, and Cuba would copy.","citation":"Lesson: From New Guinea to the New World"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What share of the global sugar market is fair trade?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Under 1 percent, but growing"},{"id":"q36b","text":"About half"},{"id":"q36c","text":"About 90 percent"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Zero; fair trade sugar does not exist"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Fair trade sugar is under 1 percent of the global market but growing, offering farmers an income premium above conventional prices. It is a small but expanding alternative.","citation":"Lesson: The Hidden Costs of Cheap Sugar"}]}
